sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 59, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 1 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT from state 0 led to state 1. Let me check what changed:
- Dark gray shape: boundary=[[18, 15], [18, 23], [20, 23], [20, 18], [21, 17], [29, 17], [29, 9], [27, 9], [27, 14], [26, 15]] - moved left by 6 cols (from cols 15-29 to cols 9-23)
- Black F: boundary=[[18, 45], [18, 53], [26, 53], [27, 54], [27, 59], [29, 59], [29, 51], [21, 51], [20, 50], [20, 45]] - didn't move!
Wait, the black F didn't move. In Level 1, LEFT moved the black F left and the dark gray F right. But here, LEFT moved the dark gray shape left and the black F stayed put.
Hmm, that's different. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- State 0: dark gray shape at cols 15-29, black F at cols 45-59
- State 1: dark gray shape at cols 9-23, black F at cols 45-59
So LEFT moved the dark gray shape left by 6 cols, but the black F didn't move. This is different from Level 1.
Wait, but in Level 1, the dark gray F and black F were on opposite sides of the vertical strip, and they moved in opposite directions. Here, the dark gray shape is on the left side and the black F is on the right side.
Maybe the mechanic is different in Level 2. Let me try RIGHT to see what happens.
Actually, let me reconsider. In Level 1:
- LEFT moved the black F left and the dark gray F right
- RIGHT moved the black F right and the dark gray F left
So the dark gray F always moved in the opposite direction of the black F.
In Level 2, LEFT moved the dark gray shape left. If the pattern holds, the black F should have moved right, but it didn't. Maybe the black F hit the right edge or something.
Wait, the black F is at cols 45-59. If it moved right by 6 cols, it would be at cols 51-65, which is beyond the board (cols 0-63). So maybe the black F hit the right edge and couldn't move.
But that doesn't explain why the dark gray shape moved left. In Level 1, LEFT moved the dark gray F right, not left.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the mechanic in Level 2 is different. Let me try RIGHT to see what happens.
Actually, looking at the image, the dark gray shape is on the left side of the vertical strip, and the black F is on the right side. In Level 1, the dark gray F was on the left and the black F was on the right initially, but after pressing LEFT, the dark gray F moved right and the black F moved left.
Wait, I need to re-examine Level 1's initial state. Let me check the history.
Actually, I don't have easy access to the initial state of Level 1. But from my earlier analysis, at the start of Level 1, the dark gray F was on the left and the black F was on the right. Pressing LEFT moved the dark gray F right and the black F left.
But in Level 2, pressing LEFT moved the dark gray shape left. This is the opposite direction!
Maybe the mechanic depends on which side of the vertical strip the shapes are on. In Level 1, the dark gray F was on the left side, and pressing LEFT moved it right (toward the center). In Level 2, the dark gray shape is on the left side, and pressing LEFT moved it left (away from the center).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the Level 1 mechanic. Let me look at the state transitions more carefully.
In Level 1, at state 18 (early state), the dark gray F was at cols 21-29 (left of center) and the black F was at cols 33-41 (right of center). Pressing LEFT moved the dark gray F right (to cols 24-32) and the black F left (to cols 30-38). So they moved toward each other.
Wait, that's not right either. Let me re-examine my earlier notes.
From my earlier analysis:
- State 18: dark gray F at cols 21-29, black F at cols 33-41
- State 19 (after LEFT): dark gray F at cols 24-29, black F at cols 30-38
So LEFT moved the dark gray F right (from cols 21-29 to cols 24-29) and the black F left (from cols 33-41 to cols 30-38). They moved toward each other.
But in Level 2:
- State 0: dark gray shape at cols 15-29, black F at cols 45-59
- State 1 (after LEFT): dark gray shape at cols 9-23, black F at cols 45-59
So LEFT moved the dark gray shape left (from cols 15-29 to cols 9-23) and the black F didn't move. They moved apart (or stayed same).
This is confusing. Let me try RIGHT to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: In Level 2, LEFT moved dark gray shape LEFT by 6 cols (cols 15-29 → 9-23), but black F didn't move. This is different from Level 1. Let me probe RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Probe RIGHT from state 1
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}, no_impact={result.get('no_impact')}, done={result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print(f"Current state: {state_graph.current}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
